I am an over 60 year old woman and I would actually like to see a movie where a woman my age manages to outmaneuver a bunch of jacked military trained bad guys... if it was done at least sort of realistically, the old woman using her brains, maybe doing a lot of MacGyvering and making traps, let's start that in the beginning she behaves in a way that makes them completely underestimate her, no manly standoffs but crying and appearing very weak and totally subdued so that they stop paying any attention to her.

But since I am an over 60 year old woman I know damn well what kind of a chance somebody near my age, well trained and in good shape for her age or not, would have against a big younger man. And yep, the only chance somebody like me would have in that situation is if she managed to fully surprise the guy and take him out before he has the chance to actually fight her, so hitting him from behind on the head with a sledgehammer, maybe yes, an extended fight using a skillet, no effing way. An old woman with good training and in excellent shape might have a chance against a younger man if he was neither, but even then her chances might get borderline fast unless she managed to do something to take him out of the fight right in the beginning. Sure we can see those youtube videos and read stories of some old female martial artists taking down much younger opponents, but fighting on a tatami is not the same as fighting on the street, and there are equally many stories of black belts losing a fight on the latter situation.

replies.author: @rpgadventurer32
replies.text:
Well, actions movies cater to men because boys and men are the core audience for action movies, always have been. It's a male dominated genre and there is nothing wrong with that. The ones who make believable on screen action heroes the most are male characters, it's an inherently masculine role because men are bigger, stronger, faster and quicker to react.  They portray the role much more realistically and authentically; they are the ones the ones who protect society and their countries in reality, they are the ones who still fight on the front lines with up to 99% in most modern armies in the West(not even taking into account the rest of the world), they are the ones who build up the infrastructure, the buildings, the roads, they are the ones who work in the most physically demanding jobs on the planet, they are the ones who literally carry society on their backs. 
Now, even with this reality you can still have your "Ellen Ripley", your "Brienne of Tarth", your "Xena warrior princess", your "Nikita" or your "Buffy the Vampire slayer", but they are an exception and they work in their roles BECAUSE they are the exception. They work as a rarity among female characters in entertainment, whether it's because of their rare physical abilities like with Brienne of Tarth (who is unique among women in that medieval world), whether it's because of high level training as special agents like with Nikita (even though she was never portrayed as invincible in fights) or because they are a fantasy character in a mythological world where both men and women can become strong warriors and generals like with Xena Warrior Princess(which was a 90's feminist power fantasy but one that appealed to everyone), or whether it's because of enhanced genetics, robotics or magical abilities like with some of the classic Marvel and DC female characters or even with Buffy. They worked in Cinema and on TV because they were well written in their roles and in the greater context of their fictional worlds. They work because they stand out and rise above the rest; it's because these strong action adventure heroines are the "exception that confirms the rule". 
More than that, what made them work was the writing and their on-screen presentation - They were not invincible, they had flaws, they were not girl bosses for the most parts and had to contend with the harsh reality of being unique in their positions, which was reflected even in their stories and that's why it worked all these years. Currently, Hollywood makes female characters who are insufferable, who act like men, have no femininity, are unrealistic in their strengths and accomplishments as well as subvert and replace strong male characters in male-centric roles and positions that do not belong to them and will never belong to them. This is why it doesn't work and can never work. Currently, modern-day DEI "girl bosses" subvert the very foundation of classic storytelling and the "hero's journey", and have characters that are unbelievable without any hint of authenticity in any of their stories, their fictional worlds, or their characters. There is a strong lack of verisimilitude in these DEI driven female characters that are created and written by current day "woke" activists within Hollywood.

replies.author: @scionofdorn9101
replies.text:
I’m not sure when she’s supposed to have served, and what branch, because I won’t watch this garbage, but that’s not necessarily true.

I served the USAF in the Gulf War, and we had women in combat roles alongside us. However, our unit was highly specialized and unique, and our combat training was (and still is) an one of a kind training program in the ENTIRETY of the USAF. Additionally, most of our training was for defense, exfiltration, counter-insurgency, peacekeeping, and light recon, as well as first aid and MOPP (anti-rad/chem/bio) procedures.

But the gals were right there beside us digging foxholes. Granted, they underperformed compared to the men to the extent that the testing requirements were demonstrably, by regulation, lower for them.

Anything the men had to do, they only had to do 1/2 to 3/4 as well. But that was true in basic training as well.

That said, NO WOMAN does what these Hollywood power-fantasies do.

replies.author: @scionofdorn9101
replies.text: @user-mj7gm6gj9v Or only in a very limited fashion, usually just to assist the men. In my unit, women could be expected to restrain, body search, and guard POWs, BUT it was almost always with at least one man present doing the bulk of the “heavy lifting”.

author: @jeffreybaker4399
text: Personally, one phrase that I am really tired of hearing is "I had to work twice as hard". What does that even mean? Yeah, yeah, some people are born with a silver spoon in their mouth. Got it. But most people who got somewhere (good or bad, nice or mean) even with a "head start" put in a lot of hours to advance. Their story is often seven days a week, 10-plus hours a day (even if it means trampling family and friends because there is no time for them).  So, twice THAT?  What, 20-hour days for years at a time? "Twice as hard" is such a hyperbolic phrase that I pretty much automatically dismiss it.

replies.author: @dontbeorbeevil
replies.text: In a real world, when you get born with average brain, you indeed have to work twice or even thrice as hard to be where talented people can get without effort. That is how life works. Not because she was not fit, not because she did not have learning abilities--because she was a woman. A world of constant oppression and hate. A world ruled by ruthless oppressors known as white men. The day we can get these people proper treatment, is the day this cancerous ideology begins to die. We have too many crazy people out there. replies.author: @imranabdur-rahman3282
replies.text:
Yes, many groups have faced hardship or enslavement at some point in history. But the transatlantic slave trade and the system of chattel slavery in the U.S. were particularly brutal, dehumanizing, and explicitly race-based. It lasted for centuries, followed by Jim Crow laws, redlining, mass incarceration, and other forms of institutional discrimination—most of which still echo today. The damage wasn’t just physical or emotional—it was economic, social, and generational.

Speaking out about real issues—like wealth inequality, discrimination in policing, and health disparities—isn’t playing the victim; it’s demanding accountability and justice. Every major movement for civil rights, from the abolition of slavery to Black Lives Matter, has been about resilience, not victimhood.

When Black Americans talk about reparations, it’s not just about slavery—it’s about centuries of compounded disadvantage that were legally enforced and economically devastating. Other groups have received reparations (e.g., Japanese Americans after internment, Holocaust survivors), so why is it “playing victim” when Black Americans raise the issue?

Other groups have demanded justice too. Indigenous people, for example, have long called for land returns and reparations. The difference is that the grievances of Black Americans are often uniquely dismissed or mocked, which reveals a bias in how we treat their demands for justice.
